coredata.dc:title: 3D-nanocubes of N-doped carbon quantum dots adorned manganese oxide: A functional electrocatalyst for the sensitive detection of sulfadiazine
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Devi, Ramadhass Keerthika
Ganesan, Muthusankar
Chen, Tse-Wei
Chen, Shen-Ming
Al-onazi, Wedad A.
Al-Mohaimeed, Amal M.
Elshikh, Mohamed S.
Yu, Yen-Yao
coredata.dc:description: Rapid monitoring of antibiotic drugs has received a lot of attention due to the great global demand for cost-effective and high-efficacy medications. Herein, a simple fabrication of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots adorned with manganese oxide nanocubes (N-CQD@Mn3O4 NCs) is reported, and their electrocatalytic activity has been used to detect the antibiotic drug sulfadiazine (SDZ). The impact of experimental parameters such as active material loading concentration, scan rate, pH, analyte accumulation duration, and SDZ concentration, were analyzed against the oxidation peak signals of SDZ. The N-CQD@Mn3O4 NCs modified electrode demonstrated a well-resolved oxidation peak at the lowest oxidation potential of 0.89 V vs. Ag/AgCl, owing to the high active sites, catalytic efficiency, and synergistic impact of N-CQDs and Mn3O4, Notably, the modified electrode exhibited excellent electrochemical performance in terms of low detection limit (0.014 µM), high sensitivity (2.75 µA µM−1 cm−2), and appreciable linear range (0.5 – 663 µM). Moreover, good reproducibility and storage stability were also achieved. Finally, the proposed sensor demonstrated its viable application by detecting the SDZ in the human urine samples with satisfactory results (〜99%).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Manganese oxide
Nanocubes
Sulfadiazine
Antibiotic drug
Electrochemical sensor

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs)
Synthesis of N-CQDs adorned Mn3O4 nanocubes (N-CQD@ Mn3O4 NCs)
Optimizing the loading concentration, loading volume, and accumulation time
Effect of concentration, scan rate, and pH
Analytical performance of N-CQD@Mn3O4/SPCE towards SDZ detection
Interference, reproducibility, and storage stability analysis

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan has financially supported this work through contract no. MOST 110-2113-M-027-003. The authors extend their appreciation to the Researchers supporting project number (RSP2022R469) King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

The Ministry of Science and Technology , Taiwan has financially supported this work through contract no. MOST 110-2113-M-027-003 . The authors extend their appreciation to the Researchers supporting project number ( RSP2022R469 ) King Saud University , Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HR-TEM images of N-CQDs at the magnifications of (a) 50 nm, and (b) 20 nm (Insert: SAED pattern). (c) FE-SEM image of N-CQD@Mn3O4 NCs, HR-TEM images of N-CQD@Mn3O4 NCs at the magnifications of (d) 500 nm, (e) 200 nm, (f) 50 nm, (g) SAED pattern, and (h) the corresponding EDS spectrum.
PXRD pattern of pristine Mn3O4 and N-CQD@Mn3O4 NCs.

(a) Nyquist plot (Insert: Randels equivalent circuit) (b) CV plot for the unmodified and modified electrodes. (c) CVs of N-CQD@Mn3O4/SPCE for the different scan rates (10 – 100 mV s−1), and its corresponding (d) linear plot. All the electrochemical characterization experiments were performed in the ferricyanide system (5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- in 0.1 M KCl).

The anodic peak current signals of SDZ (200 μM) at (a) different dosages of N-CQD@Mn3O4/SPCE, (b) various loading volumes of N-CQD@Mn3O4/SPCE, and (c) the effect of SDZ accumulation time at the surface of N-CQD@Mn3O4/SPCE.

(a) CV responses of bare and modified electrodes towards the electrooxidation of SDZ in PB (pH 7.0, N2 saturated) at the scan rate of 50 mV s−1. (b) Possible electro-oxidation mechanism of SDZ at the surface of N-CQD@Mn3O4/SPCE.

By using the N-CQD@Mn3O4/SPCE, (a) CVs for the increasing concentration of SDZ (50 – 250 μM), (b) the corresponding linear plot of concentration vs. Ipa, and (c) log concentration vs. log Ipa. (d) CVs for increasing scan rate from 20 to 140 mV s−1, using N-CQD@Mn3O4/SPCE with the existence of 200 μM SDZ, (e) the corresponding linear plot of the square root of scan rate vs. Ipa, and (f) log scan rate vs. log Ipa.
(a) The effect of various pH (3.0 – 11.0) on the SDZ oxidation at the surface of N-CQD@Mn3O4/SPCE, (b) a plot of pH vs. Ipa, and (c) calibration plot of pH vs. Epa.

(a) DPV responses of N-CQD@Mn3O4/SPCE for the various concentration of SDZ (0.5–663 μM) (b) linear calibration plot between SDZ concentration and oxidation peak current response (c) DPV responses of N-CQD@Mn3O4/SPCE towards SDZ in the co-existence of interfering molecules, and (d) the corresponding relative error% for the detection of SDZ in the presence of interferents.
Graphical representation for the synthesis of N-CQD@Mn3O4 NCs, and its electrochemical application.
Comparison table for the analytical results of N-CQD@Mn3O4/SPCE with the previously reported electrocatalysts for the detection of SDZ.
Recovery results in human urine samples using a developed SDZ sensor.

head.ce:title: 3D-nanocubes of N-doped carbon quantum dots adorned manganese oxide: A functional electrocatalyst for the sensitive detection of sulfadiazine

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rapid monitoring of antibiotic drugs has received a lot of attention due to the great global demand for cost-effective and high-efficacy medications. Herein, a simple fabrication of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots adorned with manganese oxide nanocubes (N-CQD@Mn3O4 NCs) is reported, and their electrocatalytic activity has been used to detect the antibiotic drug sulfadiazine (SDZ). The impact of experimental parameters such as active material loading concentration, scan rate, pH, analyte accumulation duration, and SDZ concentration, were analyzed against the oxidation peak signals of SDZ. The N-CQD@Mn3O4 NCs modified electrode demonstrated a well-resolved oxidation peak at the lowest oxidation potential of 0.89 V vs. Ag/AgCl, owing to the high active sites, catalytic efficiency, and synergistic impact of N-CQDs and Mn3O4, Notably, the modified electrode exhibited excellent electrochemical performance in terms of low detection limit (0.014 µM), high sensitivity (2.75 µA µM−1 cm−2), and appreciable linear range (0.5 – 663 µM). Moreover, good reproducibility and storage stability were also achieved. Finally, the proposed sensor demonstrated its viable application by detecting the SDZ in the human urine samples with satisfactory results (〜99%).

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotic exposure in the environment is still being intensely viewed as a major global health issue. Antibiotic resistance is growing increasingly widespread as a result of inappropriate antibiotic usage and poor antibiotic removal. It is currently regarded as one of the world’s top then health hazards by the world health organization (WHO) [1]. It is consequently vital to identify management measures that can restrict the spread of antibiotic contamination in order to limit its effects on the environment [2]. Sulfa-based antibiotics, often known as sulfonamides, have a structural similarity to sulfanilamide. They are frequently employed in veterinary medicine for the treatment of illnesses such as pneumonia, meningitis, urinary tract infections, and bronchitis, emphasizing their widespread usage throughout diverse ecological zones [3]. Sulfadiazine (SDZ, 4-amino-N-2-pyrimidinylbenzenesulfonamide) is a kind of sulfonamide antibiotic that is often administrated in human and veterinary medicine for toxoplasmosis, wound sepsis, nocardiosis, etc [4]. However, excessive SDZ use and inappropriate disposal have polluted the ecosystem. Furthermore, the harmful effects of SDZ accumulation and its persistence in soil and aquatic systems cause chronic toxicity, liver damage, and gene damage in all living organisms [5]. In China, the maximum allowable values of SDZ in aquatic environments range from 0.014 to 0.28 μg/L [6]. The highest permissible content of SDZ in milk, according to international regulations, is 0.07 ppm [7]. As a result, the easy and rapid trace-level quantification of SDZ in environmental and biological samples has gained increasing interest.

High-performance liquid chromatography [8], capillary electrophoresis [9], UV–visible spectroscopy, and Tandem mass spectrometry [10], are some of the methods adopted for the SDZ determination. Electrochemical methods, on the other hand, appear to outperform these approaches owing to the advantages of low-cost, user-friendly, high selectivity, sensitivity, and portability. By the surface modification of electrodes, the analytical performance of the electrochemical system can be boosted by the addition of additional specific chemical characteristics. Optimal performance is achieved by critical surface modification, which provides a significant drop in peak potential, excellent sensitivity, low detection limit, and fast electron transfer kinetics. The designs of electrode modification with functionalized nanomaterials, stimulate attempts to produce novel electrocatalysts and are essential to achieving an improved electrochemical system. Thus, to increase the performance of the electrochemical sensor, still, tremendous research has been focused on novel, green, and economical nanomaterials. In the class of transition metal oxides (TMOs), manganese oxide (MnO), being one of the most prevalent non-precious materials, has a variety of morphologies (including; nanowires, nanoneedles, nanocrystal, multipods, and dendritic structure) [11] and is widely used in catalysis, energy storage, energy conversion, and sensors [12,13]. Particularly, the hausmannite (Mn3O4) is a stable phase of MnOs, stands out for its excellent performance in molecular adsorption and catalytic activity in electrochemical applications, which is also supported by a density-functional theory calculation, and its catalytic activity depends on its structure [14]. Thus, by fine controlling the morphology and elemental composition probably, the activity of MnOs could be optimized. Mn3O4 has the potential to be an intriguing promoter of electrocatalysis processes, but its poor electrical conductivity restricts practical usage. According to the literature, making composites with highly conductive materials, such as carbon nanotubes [15], carbon nanowall [16], reduced graphene oxide [17], graphene [18], and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [19], is a hopeful approach for increasing the electrical conductivity of Mn3O4, and would improve the electrochemical performance.

In particular, if the Mn3O4 is combined with the CQDs, various beneficial features can be achieved in a single action. CQDs are a unique form of carbon-nanostructure that has been utilized in various electrochemical applications [20]. Furthermore, nitrogen (N) doping has been demonstrated to improve the electrocatalytic performance of CQDs. According to recent research, N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs), and their composites exhibit an extraordinary electrocatalytic performance [19,21–24]. It is worth mentioning that upon introducing the nitrogen or nitrogen-containing nanomaterials, the transition metal’s d-band filled state gets narrower and the unfilled state becomes broader [19]. As result, composite of transition metal show greater catalytic activity than their parent metal or the equivalent TMOs, which may be attributed to processes involving the donation of bonding electrons. The N-CQDs have a great tendency to confine the TMOs nanostructure, and also prevent the material passivation. A functional electrocatalyst must have a large surface area and sufficient electrical conductivity. Thus, the nanoscale size of N-CQDs (〜2–10 nm) can significantly enhance the surface area of Mn3O4, hence increasing the number of accessible catalytic sites. Therefore, the design of a simple and viable process for preparing Mn3O4 based composites will be of great significance.

From these perspectives, we designed the N-CQDs adorned Mn3O4 nanocubes (N-CQD@Mn3O4 NCs) using a simple synthesis approach. The nanomaterial with a cubic shape features sharp edges and corners and thereby offers enhanced catalytic activity to nanoparticles [25]. Thus, it is anticipated that the new electrochemical sensor based on N-CQD@Mn3O4 NCs will provide decent performance in antibiotic drug SDZ, owing to the good conductivity and electrocatalytic properties of the material. Finally, the sensor’s performance parameters were well optimized to detect the SDZ in real samples.

Manganese(II) nitrate tetrahydrate (Mn(NO3)2.4 H2O), urea (CO(NH2)2), citric acid (CA, C6H8O7), propylene diamine (C2H4(NH2)2), sulfadiazine (SDZ, C10H10N4O2S), and interfering molecules used in the work were the analytical grade reagents, purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and utilized in their original form. Using methanol, a stock solution of SDZ (10 mM) was prepared, and all the remaining solutions were prepared using water (≧18 MΩ cm, Millipore system). The phosphate buffer (PB, a mixture of NaH2PO4.2 H2O and Na2HPO4.2 H2O) solution was an electrolyte for the electrochemical experiments. The material characterization and types of equipment are drafted in the Supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Our previous research was used to guide the N-CQDs synthesis procedure [21,23,24]. In a short, 50 mL of DI water was combined with citric acid (2 g), and propylene diamine (10 mL). It was then autoclaved for 4 h at 180 ºC. The obtained pale-yellow color solution was then diluted with 100 mL water and centrifuged at 10000 rpm, to eliminate the big particles. The remaining supernatant solution was filtered with a membrane having a pore size of 0.22 µm, to get fine particles of N-CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N-CQDs adorned Mn3O4 nanocubes (N-CQD@ Mn3O4 NCs)
ce:section.ce:para: The material was synthesized using the technique described elsewhere, with minor modifications. About 1.8 g of Mn(NO3)2.4 H2O, 3.45 g of CO(NH2)2, and 0.5 g of cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) were dissolved in 70 mL of water and agitated well. Using 25% ammonia water, the pH of the solution was adjusted to 8. To the mixture solution, 15 mL of N-CQDs were added and stirred for 30 mins before being transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave (100 mL). Then the precursor solution in the autoclave was heated for 12 h at 120 ºC. After naturally cooling to ambient temperature, the obtained precipitate was separated and recovered using centrifugation (5000 rpm) and then dried at 80 ºC, overnight. Then the dry powder was thermally annealed for 5 h at 600 ºC in an N2 atmosphere to get the N-CQDs@Mn3O4 NCs. In the absence of N-CQDs, pristine Mn3O4 was also synthesized for comparison.

Ultrasonication was used to disperse about 10 mg (optimized concentration) of N-CQD@Mn3O4 nanocomposite in 1 mL of isopropanol. About 6.0 µL (optimized volume) of the resultant suspension was pipetted out and loaded on the SPCE and dried at room temperature. This modified SPCE is now known as N-CQD@Mn3O4/SPCE, and it is used in electrochemical experiments to detect SDZ. Scheme 1 depicts the synthesis, electrode modification process, and electrochemical applications of N-CQD@Mn3O4 NCs.

The particle size and surface morphology of N-CQDs, and N-CQD@Mn3O4 NCs were characterized using HR-TEM analysis, and their portraits are shown in Fig. 1. The N-CQDs appear to be spherical particles with a homogenous size distribution (Fig. 1a, b). The typical particle size of the N-CQDs ranges between 8 and 10 nm on average. Furthermore, the SAED pattern of N-CQD (insert: Fig. 1a) shows a few bright spots and rings, which are associated with crystalline and amorphous natures of N-CQDs, respectively.

Moreover, the EDS spectrum of N-CQDs (Fig. S1a), confirms the presence of elements such as carbon (C), nitrogen (N), and oxygen (O), in its core structure. As a result, it is also confirmed that the CQDs are effectively doped with the heteroatom (N) in their matrix. HR-TEM images of N-CQD@Mn3O4 NCs (Fig. 1d-f) depict the perfect 3D-cube shape morphology, and the corresponding SAED pattern (Fig. 1g) emphasizes good crystalline arrangements of the N-CQD@Mn3O4 NCs. The NCs are evenly shaped and the average size (edge length) of the particles is 150 nm. Almost all particle surface shows an orientation close to {100} facets of a cubic structure. The possible reason for the structural formation of N-CQD@Mn3O4 nanocubes is as follows; N-CQDs have the various functional groups on their surface that act as an anchoring point and readily attach with the manganese ions. Then during the hydrothermal process, the captured ions are nucleated as a manganese oxide. Due to the presence of N-CQDs, each nucleolus is separated from one another, grows in a controlled manner and end up with the nanocubic structure without any agglomeration. Further, the magnified HR-TEM images (Fig. 1f) show the spherical dots found to be scattered over the cubic matrix are nothing but N-CQDs. The elemental composition of N-CQD@Mn3O4 NCs was assessed by EDS analysis (Fig. 1h), which confirms the presence of elements such as Mn, O, C and N. The found other element copper (Cu) is emerged from the sample containing Cu grid, during the HR-TEM analysis. Thus, the HR-TEM analysis confirms the successful incorporation of N-CQDs over the cubic matrix of Mn3O4.

A thin film X-ray diffraction of N-CQDs (Fig. S1b) shows the broad diffraction between 2θ of 20° and 30°, indicating that the N-CQDs are amorphous in nature. PXRD patterns of the pristine Mn3O4 and N-CQD@Mn3O4 NCs are shown in Fig. 2. The diffraction peaks at a 2θ angle of 18.2°, 26. 3°, 28.8°, 31.1°, 32.4°, 36. 3°, 38.0°, 44.5°, 50.8°, 53.9°, 58.7°, 60.0°, and 64.7° are exhibited by both pristine Mn3O4 and N-CQD@Mn3O4 NCs. The observed diffraction peaks are corresponding to the crystal planes (101), (211), (112), (200), (103), (202), (004), (220), (105), (312), (321), (215) and (400), respectively, and well harmonized with the standard data (JCPDS #01 −089 −4837) of tetragonal - hausmannite structure.

Additionally, N-CQD/Mn3O4 NCs show a broad peak between 20° and 30°, which is due to the amorphous nature of N-CQDs. It verifies that N-CQDs are successfully incorporated with the Mn3O4 matrix, similar to the literature reports [21,22,24]. Thus, the XRD pattern confirms that the N-CQD@Mn3O4 NCs are in good crystallinity and the absence of impurity peaks also suggests the purity of the material.

The electrochemical surface properties of bare SPCE, N-CQD/SPCE, Mn3O4/SPCE, and N-CQD@Mn3O4/SPCE were examined using an EIS in a standard probe solution (0.1 M KCl solution containing 5 mM of [Fe(CN)6]3-/4 system). Before the analysis, the probe solution was completely de-oxygenated with nitrogen gas (N2) before the analysis.

This process ensures the high sensitivity of the redox probes and thoroughly examines the surface characteristics. The electrode's EIS outcomes are presented as a Nyquist plot in Fig. 3a (Insert: equivalent circuit). The semicircles shown in the EIS graphs represent the charge transfer resistance (Rct) value of the electrodes. Higher electrical conductivity is represented by a lower Rct value. The measured Rct values, 205, 181, 107, and 72, respectively, correspond to the bare SPCE, Mn3O4/SPCE, N-CQD/SPCE, and N-CQD@Mn3O4/SPCE. As result, the Rct value of N-CQD@Mn3O4/SPCE is low, indicating that the electron transport across the electrode/electrolyte interface is faster than other compared electrodes. Furthermore, using the respective Rct values of the modified electrodes, their charge-transfer rate constant (Ks) is also determined using the following equation (Eq. 1) [26].
(1)
R
ct
=
RT
n
2
F
2
K
s
C

where n and C are the transferred number of electrons, and concentration of probe solution, respectively. The remaining symbols in Eq. 1 have their common meanings. The Ks values for bare SPCE, Mn3O4/SPCE, N-CQD/SPCE, and N-CQD@Mn3O4/SPCE are 2.1 × 10−8, 2.5 × 10−8, 4.2 × 10−8, and 6.2 × 10−8, respectively. The results show that the charge-transfer rate constant of the N-CQD@Mn3O4/SPCE is the highest. As a result of the findings, the N-CQD@Mn3O4/SPCE may provide excellent electrical contact to the electrode/electrolyte interface, as well as a robust electrocatalytic reaction to electroactive species, which are both very desired features for electrochemical sensors.

Further, the electrochemical behavior of bare SPCE and modified SPCEs were examined by CV in a standard probe solution at the scanning rate of 50 mV s−1. The comparison CV curves of the various electrodes in the standard redox system are shown in Fig. 3b. The electrodes show a pair of redox peaks with the anodic and cathodic peak separation (∆E p) values of 250, 240, 223, and 207 mV, for bare SPCE, Mn3O4/SPCE, N-CQD/SPCE, and N-CQD@Mn3O4/SPCE, respectively. The peak separation value is inversely proportional to the electron transfer rate. Thus, the low ∆E p of N-CQD@Mn3O4/SPCE confirms the fast electron transfer behavior than other compared electrodes. Further N-CQD@Mn3O4/SPCE was subjected to the different scan rate studies (Fig. 3c) in a standard probe solution to estimate its electrochemical surface area (ECSA). The increase in scanning rate from 10 to 100 mV s−1 leads to the increase in redox peaks. A good linear relationship was obtained when drawing the plot of redox peaks currents vs. the square root of the scan rate (Fig. 3d), with high correlation coefficients (R2 = 0.9995 (for Ipa) and 0.9993 (for Ipc)), suggesting the diffusion-controlled electrode process of the ferricyanide system at the electrode surface. Further, the ECSA was calculated using the Randles−Sevcik equation (Eq. 2) [21,22,27].
(2)
I
p
= ( 2.69 ×
10
5
)
n
3 / 2
D
1 / 2
ν
1 / 2
A
C
*

Where I
p
, D, ν, n, A, and C* denote the anodic peak current, diffusion coefficient, scanning rate, number of electrons transferred, surface area, and concentration of [Fe(CN)6]3-/4, respectively. The calculated ECSA of N-CQD@Mn3O4/SPCE is 0.1176 cm2 with respect to the real surface area of GCE (geometric area ~ 0.07 cm2). Based on the findings, it is possible to conclude that N-CQD@Mn3O4/SPCE has a better electron transport channel and a larger surface area thangbare SPCE, and it can begin effective electrode material for electrochemical sensor applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimizing the loading concentration, loading volume, and accumulation time
ce:section.ce:para: The loading concentration and volume of the N-CQD@Mn3O4 NCs were tuned against the SDZ oxidation current response in order to use them as an effective electrocatalyst. The effect of electrocatalyst concentration is shown in Fig. 4a, which shows that the oxidation current increases as the concentration increases from 5 to 10 mg mL−1. However, if the concentration exceeds 10 mg mL−1, the current response steadily decreases. It may be caused by the formation of a thick electrocatalyst layer over the electrode surface, and it may reduce the diffusion of charges [27,28]. Therefore, 10 mg mL−1 of N-CQD@Mn3O4 NCs suspension has been chosen as the optimum electrocatalyst concentration for SDZ detection. As similar, at the optimum concentration, N-CQD@Mn3O4 NCs suspension’s loading volume from 4.0 to 10.0 µL was examined, and the results are displayed in Fig. 4b. The oxidation current response of SDZ becomes high at 6.0 µL than at other loading volumes.

Hence, the electrocatalyst with a 6.0 µL loading volume was used for further electrochemical studies. A study of accumulation time can advance the determination of SDZ at the modified electrode N-CQD@Mn3O4/SPCE. Therefore, the effect of accumulation time for the oxidation of SDZ was examined by CV in the presence of 200 µM SDZ in PBS (pH 7.0, N2 saturated) with a 50 mV s−1 scanning rate. The increase in the accumulation time from 0 to 150 s and the corresponding anodic peak current of SDZ are shown in Fig. 4c. The results indicate at 90 s of accumulation time only the maximum current response was achieved. Further increment of accumulation time leads to the constant oxidation peak current, indicating the sensitivity of SDZ on the electrode surface approach to a limiting value. Thus, the accumulation time of 90 s was the optimum condition for the sensitive detection of SDZ using N-CQD@Mn3O4/SPCE.

CV curves of the bare SPCE, N-CQD/SPCE, Mn3O4/SPCE, and N-CQD@ Mn3O4/SPCE in the presence of 200 µM SDZ in PB (pH 7.0, N2 saturated) at 50 mV s−1 are shown in Fig. 5a to differentiate the electrochemical responses of unmodified and modified electrodes. The bare SPCE exhibits a weak anodic peak at 0.97 V with a very low oxidation current response (3.5 μA) during SDZ oxidation. While N-CQD/SPCE has a better peak current responsiveness (8.5 μA) with a lower oxidation potential (0.95 V) than bare SPCE. It is related to N-CQDs advantageous features, such as excellent electrical conductivity, and strong catalytic activity on the electrode surface [23,24].

Besides, the Mn3O4/SPCE oxidizes SDZ at a lower oxidation potential (0.88 V with 6.3 μA) than bare SPCE, and N-CQD/SPCE, indicating its good electrocatalytic nature. However, it did not exhibit a better current response than N-CQD/GCE, showing the moderate conducting nature of the Mn3O4/SPCE. In contrast, the N-CQD@Mn3O4/SPCE had a better and improved current response (11.3 μA at 0.88 V) for SDZ oxidation than the other compared electrodes. Because, in terms of synergistic impact, numerous active sites, and strong catalytic activity, the inclusion of N-CQDs across the surface of the Mn3O4 matrix offers significant advantages for SDZ electrochemical detection. The peak anodic current of the N-CQD@Mn3O4/SPCE is, 3.2, 1.3, and 1.8-times greater than that of bare SPCE, N-CQD/SPCE, and Mn3O4/SPCE, respectively. The overall electrochemical oxidation mechanism for SDZ detection is displayed in Fig. 5b. The SDZ is directly oxidized by an irreversible transfer of two protons, and two electrons (2 H+, 2e-). Thus, the related electrochemical findings demonstrated that the N-CQD@Mn3O4/SPCE had stronger electrocatalytic activity than other compared electrodes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of concentration, scan rate, and pH
ce:section.ce:para: The modified electrode N-CQD@Mn3O4 has been further subjected to electrochemical scrutinization such as the impact of concentration, and scan rates, on the SDZ oxidation current response. First, the SDZ concentration was raised from 50 to 250 µM, and their oxidation current responses (Ipa) at pH 7.0 (scan rate: 50 mV s−1) were evaluated, as presented in Fig. 6a. The Ipa value steadily improved as SDZ concentration raised, with no deformation or overlapping of the peak response. As a result, it proves that the nanocomposite is antifouling and that increase in the concentration of SDZ would not impair its electrochemical performance. Further the linear relationship between SDZ concentration and Ipa (Fig. 6b) can be represented as follows; Ipa (µA) = 0.0414 Cg (µM) + 5.123 with R2 = 0.9953. in addition, the log SDZ concentration vs. log Ipa calibration plot (Fig. 6c) also gives linear plot, with the linear regression equation of log Ipa (µA) = 0.7014 log C (µM) + 0.0679 (R2 = 0.9874). The fact that the matching slope value (0.7014) is nearly equivalent to 1 suggests that electron transport occurs via the first-order kinetic process.

The electrochemical reaction kinetics at the surface of N-CQD@Mn3O4/SPCE can be easily derived from CV experiments at various scan speeds. Here the different scan rate experiment was performed from 20 to 140 mV s−1, with 200gµM SDZ in PB (pH 7.0, N2 saturated), as shown in Fig. 6d. The SDZ’s Ipa value gradually grew as the scan rate increased from low to high. While the oxidation potential also slightly shifted towards the more positive potential side. Only the plot of the square root of the scan rate vs. Ipa, rather than the scan rate vs. Ipa, revealed a good linear connection between the scan rate and Ipa (Fig. 6e). The corresponding linear regression equation is Ipa (µA) = 2.7445 v (mV s−1) – 4.6776; (R2 = 0.9982). Moreover, a plot of log scan rate vs. log current (Fig. 6f) also shows the linear relationship which can be expressed as follows; log Ipa (µA) = 0.7565 log v (mV s−1) – 0.0426; (R2 = 0.9919). The results show that the diffusion process is primarily controlling the electro-oxidation of SDZ at the surface of N-CQD@Mn3O4/SPCE.

The electrolyte’s pH plays a significant role in the sensor’s performance. Thus, to examine the effect of pH for SDZ detection using N-CQD@Mn3O4/SPCE, the PB buffer’s pH condition was varied from pH 3.0–11. 0, and the experiment was carried out with 200 μM SDZ (scan rate: 50 mV s−1), and the corresponding CVs are shown in Fig. 7a. It is observed that an increase in pH, shifts the oxidation peak potential towards the negative side, and attains a maximum current response at pH 7.0. Due to the protonation/deprotonation process of SDZ, increasing the pH value (9.0 and 11.0) causes a drop in oxidation peak current response. The corresponding CVs at different pH values indicated that pH 7.0 gave the largest oxidation peak current, which was employed as an optimum pH condition for further electrochemical experiments. The subsequent calibration plot ofgpH vs. oxidation peak current and linear plot of pH vs. oxidation potential (E
pc ) are shown in Fig. 7b and Fig. 7c, respectively, and the corresponding linear regression equation is as follows, E
pa (V) = −0.0394 (pH) + 1.016; R2 = 0.9977. The resulted slope value is near to the theoretical Nernstian slope value (−59 mV/pH), indicating that an equal number of electrons and protons are involved in the oxidation of SDZ [29].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of N-CQD@Mn3O4/SPCE towards SDZ detection
ce:section.ce:para:
Under the optimum electrochemical conditions, DPV analysis was performed using N-CQD@Mn3O4/SPCE for the sensitive detection of SDZ. The DPV method is more sensitive and offers high resolution in electrochemical signals when compared to the CV technique. The DPV responses of N-CQD@Mn3O4/SPCE with varying concentrations of SDZ (0.5 – 663 µM) were carried out in PB (pH 7.0, N2 saturated), as displayed in Fig. 8a. On increasing the SDZ concentration from low to high, a well-defined oxidation peak current response was achieved. The calibration plot between the SDZ concentration and DPV current response shows the two linear plots (Fig. 8b) ranging from 0.5 to 123 µM and 183–633 µM, with the linear regression equation of Ipa (µA) = 0.215 C [µM] + 1.1594; R2 = 0.9945, and Ipa (µA)= 0.0318 C [µM] + 18.83; R2 = 0.9912, respectively. Then the limit of detection (LOD) and sensitivity of the N-CQD@Mn3O4/SPCE towards SDZ detection were calculated using the following equations;
LOD =
3
× ( standard
deviation )
slope
value
Sensitivity =
solpe
value
of
calbiration
plot
Surface
area

The slope value from the low linear range (0.5–123 µM) was used for the calculation, and the measured LOD and sensitivity of about 0.014 µM, and 2.75 µA µM−1gcm−2, respectively. Though the two linear ranges phenomena have already been reported [29,30], we only achieved a decent LOD value with high sensitivity for SDZ detection. The achieved analytical results (LOD, sensitivity, and linear range) of N-CQD@Mn3O4/SPCE towards SDZ detection are compared with the previously reported sensing protocols, which are summarized in Table 1. As seen from the comparison table, it is obvious that the fabricated N-CQD@Mn3O4/SPCE has a superior electrocatalytic behaviour to SDZ detection. This enhanced performance of N-CQD@Mn3O4 NCs can be attributed to its unique structural feature and its surface functional groups, which offer several active sites. Thus, synergistically, the N-CQD@Mn3O4 NCs modified electrode boosts the electrochemical signals for SDZ detection. Thus, it can be a viable electrode material for SDZ detection for further development.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interference, reproducibility, and storage stability analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The DPV technique was utilized to investigate the interference analysis, reproducibility, and storage stability studies. First, the interference analysis was performed using N-CQD@Mn3O4/SPCE in PB (pH 7.0, N2gsaturated) with the existence of 100 µM SDZ, and the co-existence of 50-fold excess concentration of common interfering molecules such as uric acid (UA), ascorbic acid (AA), glucose (Glu), tryptophan (TRP), and two commonly prescribed antibiotic drugs, isoniazid (ISN), and zonisamide (ZNA). The DPV responses of SDZ, in the presence of interferents, are shown in Fig. 8c. It depicts that the common interferents caused the shifts in oxidation peak current response of SDZ, but the oxidation peak potential of SDZ remains unchanged. The relative error percentage for the detection of SDZ using N-CQD@Mn3O4/SPCE is shown in Fig. 8d. Only a 〜13% relative error only calculated for the SDZ detection, suggesting that the N-CQD@Mn3O4/SPCE has a decent selectivity for the detection of SDZ.

The reproducibility of the as-prepared N-CQD/Mn3O4/SPCE was checked by making five different N-CQD/Mn3O4/SPCE modified electrodes with the same procedure. Then the DPV was carried out in presence of 100 µM of SDZ in PB (pH 7.0, N2 saturated), and the results are shown in Fig. S2a. The results depict that all the fabricated electrodes exhibited the almost same current response with the RSD value of 1.37%, which signifies the good reproducibility of the modified electrode for SDZ detection. Then, the storage stability of the fabricated N-CQD/Mn3O4/SPCE was tested for 30 days with an interval of 5 days, and their DPV results are shown in Fig. S2b. Even after the 30 days of testing, almost 94% of SDZ oxidation peak current response was achieved, suggesting the fabricated sensor’s decent storage stability. Thus, it demonstrates that the N-CQD/Mn3O4/SPCE can perform with good performance for SDZ detection in practical applications.

The real sample analysis was performed in human urine samples. The urine samples (U1, and U2) were received from the healthy volunteers, which were centrifuged (6000 rpm) to remove any suspended particles. The obtained supernatant solution was utilized for the experiment within 24 h. To the pre-treated urine samples, buffer solution (PB, pH 7.0) was mixed at a ratio of 1:100, to make the dilution. Then SDZ with known concentrations were spiked into the diluted samples and subjected to the DPV analysis. By using the standard addition method, the spiked SDZ concentration in urine samples was examined. The obtained recovery results are presented in Table 2 and also compared with the HPLC analysis results. The obtained recovery results are in good agreement with the HPLC analysis results. The electroanalytic findings show that the fabricated sensor recovers around 98% of the SDZ in urine samples, and the RSD value for the repeating trials is calculated to be 2.3% only. Thus, the results demonstrate that the proposed sensor has a good recovery efficiency in real samples, proving its suitability for practical applications.

In summary, a simple and effective strategy for the synthesis of nanocubes structured N-CQD@Mn3O4 nanocomposite has been reported for the first time. The HR-TEM images confirm that the Mn3O4 surface matrix is well functionalized with the N-CQDs. The surface functionalization enhanced the surface area and the number of active sites of the nanocomposite. In electrochemical analysis, the N-CQD@Mn3O4 nanocomposite modified electrode exhibited a meaningfully enhanced SDZ oxidation current response to the compared electrodes. Due to the good electrocatalytic nature of the nanocomposite, which was obtained from its unique structural, and functional advantages. Further, the proposed sensor renders a low detection limit, and high sensitivity, with good repeatability and storage stability. Moreover, good recovery results in real sample analysis emphasize the proposed sensor’s viability for practical applications.

Keerthika Devi Ramadhass: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Muthusankar Ganesan: Conceptualization, Software, Writing – review & editing. Yen-Yao Yu: Data curation, Resources. Tse-Wei Chen: Data curation, Visualization Wedad A. Al-onazi: Data curation, Visualization. Amal M. Al-Mohaimeed: Data curation, Visualization. Mohamed S Elshikh: Data curation, Visualization. Shen-Ming Chen: Resources, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan has financially supported this work through contract no. MOST 110-2113-M-027-003. The authors extend their appreciation to the Researchers supporting project number (RSP2022R469) King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2022.129141.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
Supplementary material
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sulfadiazine determination in pharmaceuticals by electrochemical reduction on a glassy carbon electrode
J. Braz. Chem. Soc.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: O.C. Braga, I. Campestrini, I.C. Vieira, A. Spinelli, Sulfadiazine determination in pharmaceuticals by electrochemical reduction on a glassy carbon electrode, J. Braz. Chem. Soc. 21 (2010) 813–820. https://doi.org/10.1590/S0103–50532010000500008.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Application of bismuth-film electrode for cathodic electroanalytical determination of sulfadiazine
Electrochim. Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: I. Campestrini, O.C. de Braga, I.C. Vieira, A. Spinelli, Application of bismuth-film electrode for cathodic electroanalytical determination of sulfadiazine, Electrochim. Acta. 55 (2010) 4970–4975. https://doi.org/10.1016/J.ELECTACTA.2010.03.105.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Voltammetric detection of sulfonamides at a poly(3-methylthiophene) electrode
Talanta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T.A.M. Msagati, J.C. Ngila, Voltammetric detection of sulfonamides at a poly(3-methylthiophene) electrode, Talanta. 58 (2002) 605–610. https://doi.org/10.1016/S0039–9140(02)00327–2.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Design and in situ synthesis of titanium carbide/boron nitride nanocomposite: Investigation of electrocatalytic activity for the sulfadiazine sensor
ACS Sustain. Chem. Eng.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Kokulnathan, E.A. Kumar, T.J. Wang, Design and in situ synthesis of titanium carbide/boron nitride nanocomposite: Investigation of electrocatalytic activity for the sulfadiazine sensor, ACS Sustain. Chem. Eng. 8 (2020) 12471–12481. https://doi.org/10.1021/ACSSUSCHEMENG.0C03281/SUPPL_FILE/SC0C03281_SI_001.PDF.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Application of multiwalled carbon nanotubes/ionic liquid modified electrode for amperometric determination of sulfadiazine
Drug Test. Anal.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Hong, Y. Zhu, J. Ma, Application of multiwalled carbon nanotubes/ionic liquid modified electrode for amperometric determination of sulfadiazine, Drug Test. Anal. 4 (2012) 1034–1039. https://doi.org/10.1002/DTA.329.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Innovative screen-printed electrodes on cork composite substrates applied to sulfadiazine electrochemical sensing
J. Electroanal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.P.M. Tavares, M.H. de Sá, M.G.F. Sales, Innovative screen-printed electrodes on cork composite substrates applied to sulfadiazine electrochemical sensing, J. Electroanal. Chem. 880 (2021) 114922. https://doi.org/10.1016/J.JELECHEM.2020.114922.
